meta.title: GLE53 AMG Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Mercedes-AMG GLE53 M256 3.0 engine — real UK costs, coolant leak recall & hybrid system insight, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-AMG GLE53 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M256 3.0-litre engine typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £3,500–£5,500. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Fitting adds £1,300–£2,000 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines can exceed £14,000.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-AMG GLE53 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M256 is a sophisticated and refined engine, but it has known issues. The most significant is a coolant leak recall affecting M256-equipped vehicles. Other common issues include 48V mild-hybrid system failures, ignition coil problems, camshaft and rocker arm wear, and fuel injector issues. Regular maintenance and proactive inspection are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG GLE53?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are coolant system leaks (formal recall on M256 engines), 48V mild-hybrid (EQ Boost) system failures, ignition coil faults, camshaft and rocker arm wear causing rattling, fuel injector problems, and 48V battery electrical issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-AMG GLE53 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £65,000 GLE53 AMG, a £7,000 recon is 11% — worth it. Against a tired £25,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% Mercedes threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-AMG GLE53 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The GLE53 AMG carries the engine code M256. It's a 3.0-litre straight-six turbo petrol engine with 48V EQ Boost mild-hybrid, used in the GLE-Class V167/C167 from 2019 onwards. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 'GLE 53' or 'AMG GLE 53'.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the coolant leak on the M256, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — coolant system repairs are covered by a formal recall on M256-equipped vehicles. If your vehicle is affected, repairs are free at authorised Mercedes-Benz dealers. The job typically takes 4–6 hours, including inspection of the entire high-pressure cooling circuit and replacement of faulty seals. If you notice any symptoms of coolant system failure (sweet smells, coolant loss, temperature warnings), stop driving immediately — continued operation can destroy your engine in under 20 minutes.

problems.title: Coolant System Leaks — M256 Engine
problems.symptoms: Sweet-smelling vapour from vents or engine bay, unexplained drops in coolant level, white crystalline residue near hoses or coolant rails, dashboard overheating warnings, erratic temperature gauge behaviour.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,000 (coolant system inspection and repair)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if coolant leak leads to engine failure)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice any of these symptoms, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: The M256 engine has a known coolant leak issue. In late 2022, Mercedes-Benz initiated a safety recall affecting S-Class W223 models with the M256 engine, impacting approximately 8,500 vehicles in the UK. The defect involves a potential coolant leak from the engine's cooling system, which could lead to complete engine failure or, in extreme cases, increase fire risk. Continued operation can destroy your engine in under 20 minutes. If your GLE53 AMG exhibits any of these symptoms, have the cooling system inspected immediately and check if your vehicle is affected by any recalls.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: 48V Mild-Hybrid (EQ Boost) System Issues
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, loss of power, rough running, battery-related error messages, failure to start, vibration on cold starts.
problems.repairCost: £500–£3,000 (48V battery or ISG replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if system failure leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The M256's 48V mild-hybrid system is a complex and sometimes problematic component. The integrated starter-generator (ISG) and 48V battery can fail, causing drivetrain error messages, loss of power, and even complete engine shutdown. Some owners report that Mercedes-AMG GLE53 models have a high incidence of 48V battery failures. If you experience any electrical or drivetrain warnings, have the hybrid system inspected promptly.

failureLink.label: Read the full 48V hybrid system guide

problems.title: Ignition Coil & Fuel System Failures
problems.symptoms: Engine hesitation during acceleration, rough idle, engine misfires, loss of power, engine warning light, difficult starting.
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,200 (ignition coils or injector replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if left untreated leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The M256 engine has known issues with ignition coils not meeting specifications. Clogged fuel injectors or fuel pump failures can lead to poor engine performance, misfires, or starting difficulties. If you experience hesitation or rough running, have the ignition and fuel systems inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full ignition system failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: GLE53 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Recall may cover it; catch it early before engine damage
Specialist repair; cheaper than engine replacement
Use quality fuel; catch it early
Catch it early before catastrophic failure
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLE53 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes-AMG service history with evidence of regular oil changes using MB 229.5 spec oil
* Coolant system inspected and any recalls completed
* No coolant leaks, rough running, or hesitation
* Post-2023 build preferred (later production may have resolved some issues)
avoidIf: * Coolant leaks with no documented repair history
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis
* 48V system error messages with no documented repair
* Poor service history — the M256 is a sophisticated engine that demands good maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLE53 AMG is powered by Mercedes-AMG's M256 3.0-litre inline-six — a sophisticated, refined engine that combines performance with mild-hybrid efficiency. It delivers 429 hp and 413 lb-ft of torque, propelling this luxury SUV from 0–60 in 5.0 seconds. However, it has known weak points: coolant system leaks (with a formal recall on M256 vehicles), 48V mild-hybrid system issues, ignition coil problems, and camshaft/rocker arm wear. These are well-understood but potentially serious issues that demand attention and budget. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained GLE53 AMG is a £60,000–£90,000 performance SUV; a neglected one is a £7,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare GLE53 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-AMG GLE53 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss, sweet smells, or white residue? (M256 coolant leak recall warning)
* Service History — including the M256 era (2019–present) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.5 spec oil
* 48V System Check — Any 48V battery or EQ Boost error messages? (mild-hybrid system warning)
* Cold Start — Listen for rough running, hesitation, or vibration (ignition, injector, or camshaft wear warning)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved coolant, hybrid, ignition, or fuel system issues?
* Recall Check — Has the coolant system recall been completed? (check DVSA vehicle recall checker)
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate coolant ingress
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
